item.title: Re: Father, I crave memes.
item.content:encoded: Not all Lazytown songs are created equally amazing, it would seem. c.c Thanks for causing me to Google that.

item.title: Father, I crave memes.
item.content:encoded:
Loewes : this mailbox is mine

AND THIS TRIAGONIAL SIGN

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this - it's like it was a natural weird art phenomenum before, but then AWCY seems to have weaponised it :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, that makes sense, as they are fond of trolling the Foundation, but it wasn't my initial takeaway from the article, hence the question.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a very different view of AWCY, from back when they were the new thing. I'll admit it's somewhat tacked on, but the idea is they're aware of the anomaly and mocking the Foundation with it. They can't actually replicate it, however.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No-vote. I understand the danger of it and stuff, and that we need more mundane-won't-kill-you-but-might-in-ridiculously-improbable-circumstances, but… It doesn't grab me, and the AWCY connection is somewhat unclear - are they aware of the phenomenon and are trying to spread it? Are they its origin and are trying to reclaim it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Monty Capulet

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Harley Kaboolet

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, cool. :O I did not know such a thing was in the making.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For the purposes of the SCP Board Game, Escape from site 19, we have made a photograph of SCP-1999. The author can legally use it per his discretion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While the USA was providing logistical support in the form of Lend and Lease from the beginning, we weren't formally an active combatant in WWII until the Pearl Harbor attack in December 1941 by the Japanese. How much immigration happened prior to that point, I don't know. For eleven of the twelve month where she immigrated, we weren't technically at war with Germany.

Note that this is from next to no real information and mostly speculation, but what if she didn't come directly from Germany? She could have left Germany for England, or a neutral country, and then immigrated to America.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, certainly, but once the war started I was pretty sure the Allies closed off their borders to most immigrants, especially German ones.

I guess maybe the US hadn't joined the war at that point?

I suppose it doesn't matter that much; it just stood out for me and I thought 'Oh, this is going to be a plot point!' - and then when it wasn't, I felt a bit let down.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What, you wouldn't want to escape under the circumstances?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Does no-one else find it odd that a German migrated to the USA in the middle of the Second World War?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hardly school fret

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To me there are enough info already. The name appears with some criteria (object art in places that a specific person visited), is mostly harmless but really annoying (seems the way AWCY act) and it's not a cliché. What else needs?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reverted to revision 10: "Addenda" is the plural of "Addendum" and is not a mistake.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel that they are declaring their piece "art" by using a motif near other art.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This definitely feels like something AWCY would produce, and the effect is just off-putting enough for an upvote.

I'd like to see what would happen if it appeared on someone's face.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I find this interesting. Certainly adds a bit of mystery.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hesitant downvote.

I thought the concept was intriguing at first — a word that appeared on random places, no one's quite sure what's going on with it. However, the part where a person thinks it's someone else's made the SCP overloaded and overdone, in my opinion. I think that if you had put a little bit more detail into where it appears and the circumstances behind its appearance, you'd have a more interesting SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: arby's gruel set

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not sure when you read this, but I should have made it clearer that it just appears.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That would be weird, but even in the original draft, it was limited to objects.

AWCY I see as saying, "Well, you took our shit, so we're gonna take everyone else's shit, only we don't care about material possessions, so let this lady have it." Exactly how much they know about the Foundation, or even the SCP (they either don't know how it works or are just using it as a symbol) is left as an exercise for the reader.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So her name just appears on stuff, or does she/someone else have to write it? I like the awcy reference, even if it does feel sort of tacked on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, I was wondering about that too… It does make more sense for it to be happening spontaniously than to actually be controllable; otherwise, AWCY would've probably just gone up to the White House and spray-painted "Loewes" on it already. …Which they seem to be trying anyway.

The limitation to museum objects also renders moot a different question I had: What if the name appeared on a person's skin? Would they consider themselves to be her property or something?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was being ballsy by doing a 24 hour challenge anyway, figured I might as well go all in. (Actually, I thought it would hide my shame when this was inevitably deleted after 48 hours. I don't know what I was thinking at the time.) At least you do not hate me personally.

…Right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The short answer is no. But you know, the original Addendum 1999-a detailed how the phenomenon came to the Foundation's attention, with the name showing up on test material for an SCP, at which point the researcher goes, "I can't use this, it's Elfie's!" Security footage and a guard stationed on site showed that the item never left the researcher's hand.

Then I decided to tighten focus and just have it affect museum objects instead of anything, and so the Addendum and the explanation went by the wayside. It would have cleared that question up. I should probably put that into the description, though, that would be a good idea. But, they just show up; that's the phenomenon. This has been your long answer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: gar me stool jet

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can't say that I like this.

I can say that I hate that you took the 1999 spot.

But I can still say that I don't like this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wait, does any instance of "Loewes" written on an object with ball-point pen or felt-tip pen, in the style of Mrs. Elfa S. Loewes' handwriting, cause the anomalous effect?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
are we cool yet
star key pool rat
mars we fool bret

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would almost say this could go into -EX later, if the AWCY thing gets far enough along. But either way, upvoting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Never seen it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How does it work now? I fear I might have made it worse.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I saw this on an episode of Dexter's Lab. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I find this comment very illuminating; I may be able to solve the problem now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't get the impression that they're slapping their name on things just for publicity's sake- most of the AWCY objects I can think of were repurposed with clear goals and/or messages in mind (the original example being Ossify Wallstreet"). I'm not seeing thah here, hence my confusion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the connection, it just could have been integrated better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Even though the AWCY? reference feels a little tacked-on, I think that works for the motif established for them as a group so far; they seem so far more focused on attaching their name to pre-existing work than creating things of their own.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is maybe the best response I could hope for. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm open to suggestions. Like Lumancer said, it is kind of tacked-on, in that I just had the idea that fucking with the Foundation like this would be something AWCY would do, and ran with it. That's what I like about GOIs, and this is my first time trying to use one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Tell me about it; that's all Google gave me for search results.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: seriously.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is so out there I had to upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ahh, my mistake, looks fine then.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Object class refers to difficulty in containment. These spontaneously appear at sporadic times and locations, so euclid is accurate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Maybe I missed something, but how exactly does this classify as Euclid? it just seems like the objects produce a feeling that you do not own the object even if you do.

So where's the danger?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked it, but you could have integrated the "Are We Cool Yet" reference a little less forcibly. regardless, I upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was OK for the most part, but what's with the AWCY reference? It just feels tacked on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No, no. It's "Lowe's." They sell home-improvement supplies.

(just kidding, upvoted)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have no business doing a Bright's Challenge for my second SCP. I probably did a million things wrong, but I'm going in with both feet first.

EDIT: I want to make very clear that I took this image myself.
